records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Designated the Governor of Bengal as the 'Governor-General of Bengal' and created an Executive Council of four members to assist him.
• Established the Supreme Court at Fort William (Calcutta) in 1774 with a Chief Justice and three judges.
• Subordinated the Governors of Bombay and Madras to the Governor-General of Bengal.
• Prohibited Company servants from engaging in private trade or accepting bribes/gifts.
• Required the Court of Directors to report on revenue, civil, and military affairs to the British Treasury.
records.Constitutional Significance: It was the first step by the British Government to regulate East India Company affairs and laid the foundations for central administration and parliamentary control in India.

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Exempted the Governor-General and Executive Council from Supreme Court jurisdiction for official acts.
• Excluded revenue matters and collection from judicial oversight.
• Recognized Sadar Diwani Adalat as a court of record; appeals from provincial courts were directed to the Governor-General-in-Council.
• Mandated the application of personal laws (Hindu/Mohammedan) in cases.
records.Constitutional Significance: Represented the first attempt to separate the executive from the judiciary by defining specific jurisdictional areas and asserting executive supremacy in revenue matters.

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Distinguished between the commercial and political functions of the Company.
• Established a six-member Board of Control to supervise political affairs while the Court of Directors managed commercial affairs.
• Termed Company territories as 'British possessions in India' for the first time.
• Reduced the number of Executive Councillors to three.
records.Constitutional Significance: Introduced the system of 'dual government' (commercial vs. political) which lasted until 1858 and established supreme British Government control over Indian administration.

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Granted the Governor-General the power to override his council in special cases.
• Governor-General to also act as Commander-in-Chief.
records.Constitutional Significance: Centralised authority by concentrating military and executive powers in the office of the Governor-General.

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Extended the East India Company's trade monopoly for another 20 years.
• Extended overriding powers to future Governor-Generals and Governors.
• Formally separated revenue administration from judicial functions.
records.Constitutional Significance: Established written laws and regulations as the foundation of government, replacing personal rule with a regular code of internal regulations.

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Abolished the Company's trade monopoly in India (except for tea and trade with China).
• Explicitly asserted the sovereignty of the British Crown over Indian territories.
• Allocated Rs. 1,00,000 annually for the promotion of education and science.
• Permitted Christian missionaries to enter the country.
records.Constitutional Significance: Marked the beginning of the end of the Company’s commercial character and initiated formal state responsibility for education and social promotion.

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Redesignated the Governor-General of Bengal as the 'Governor-General of India' with full civil and military powers.
• Deprived the Governors of Bombay and Madras of their legislative powers.
• Ended the Company’s activities as a commercial body, making it a purely administrative body.
• Established the first Law Commission under Lord Macaulay to codify Indian laws.
records.Constitutional Significance: Represented the final step towards administrative and legislative centralisation in British India, creating a uniform legislative framework.

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Separated the legislative and executive functions of the Governor-General's Council.
• Created the Indian Legislative Council (Mini-Parliament) with six new members.
• Introduced a system of open competition for the selection and recruitment of civil servants.
• Introduced local representation in the Central Legislative Council.
records.Constitutional Significance: Marked the beginning of a modern parliamentary system in India and the first formal step toward the separation of powers.

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Transferred power from the East India Company to the British Crown.
• Created the office of Secretary of State for India, assisted by a 15-member Council.
• Redesignated the Governor-General as the Viceroy of India.
• Abolished the Board of Control and Court of Directors, ending the system of 'dual government'.
records.Constitutional Significance: Established absolute imperial control and direct responsibility of the British Crown for Indian governance (the British Raj).

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Introduced representative institutions by nominating Indians as non-official members to the Legislative Council.
• Restored legislative powers to the Bombay and Madras Presidencies (initiating decentralisation).
• Recognized the 'Portfolio System' (cabinet-style executive).
• Empowered the Viceroy to issue ordinances during emergencies.
records.Constitutional Significance: Initiated the process of legislative decentralisation and provided the foundation for representative governance in India.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • Aimed to curtail the freedom of the Indian-language (vernacular) press.
records.Constitutional Significance: Significant for the history of civil liberties and the struggle for press freedom under British rule.

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Increased the number of non-official members in central and provincial councils.
• Introduced the principle of elections in an indirect manner via local body recommendations.
• Empowered Legislative Councils to discuss the budget and address questions to the executive.
records.Constitutional Significance: Expanded the functions and representative nature of the legislatures, introducing the first limited elective element in governance.

records.Governor-General or Key Figure: Lord Minto; John Morley; Satyendra Prasad Sinha
records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Introduced 'separate electorates' for Muslims, legally recognising communal representation.
• Increased the size of legislative councils (Central Council from 16 to 60 members).
• Allowed Satyendra Prasad Sinha to become the first Indian to join the Viceroy’s Executive Council.
• Formally accepted the principle of election for council seats.
records.Constitutional Significance: Institutionalised communalism ('Divide and Rule') in the electoral system and expanded Indian participation in administration.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • Co-founded to tackle social and economic issues faced by women workers.
records.Constitutional Significance: Initiated formal collective advocacy for women's rights and social equality within the pre-constitutional framework.

records.Act or Agreement Name: Government of India Act of 1919 (Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms)
records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Introduced 'Dyarchy' in the provinces (dividing subjects into transferred and reserved).
• Established a bicameral legislature at the centre (Council of State and Legislative Assembly).
• Introduced direct elections with limited franchise and extended separate electorates to Sikhs, Christians, and Europeans.
• Separated provincial and central budgets and established a Public Service Commission.
records.Constitutional Significance: Introduced the concept of responsible government at the provincial level and expanded the foundation for parliamentary democracy.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • Large-scale non-violent protest against British salt laws and tax policies with mass participation of women.
records.Constitutional Significance: Increased the pressure for total independence and broader franchise, highlighting the need for inclusive constitutional drafting.

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Abandoned separate electorates for 'Depressed Classes' in favour of reserved seats within the Hindu joint electorate.
• Reserved 148 seats in provincial legislatures and 18% of seats in the Central Legislature for Depressed Classes.
• Called for non-discrimination in public services and educational grants for the community.
records.Constitutional Significance: Preserved Hindu social unity while securing guaranteed political representation for Scheduled Castes, directly influencing the 1935 Act and modern reservation policies.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • The first official demand for a Constituent Assembly for India was put forward.
records.Constitutional Significance: Initiated the formal political demand for a representative body to draft an indigenous constitution for India.

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Proposed an All-India Federation of provinces and princely states (never implemented).
• Abolished provincial dyarchy and introduced 'Provincial Autonomy'.
• Introduced dyarchy at the federal centre and divided powers into three lists (Federal, Provincial, Concurrent).
• Established the Federal Court (1937), the Reserve Bank of India (RBI), and Public Service Commissions.
records.Constitutional Significance: Served as the primary structural blueprint for the 1950 Constitution, introducing the federal principle and division of powers.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • Proposed Dominion status, expansion of the Viceroy's executive council, and the setting up of a constituent assembly after the war.
records.Constitutional Significance: Represented the first time the British Government formally accepted the principle that Indians should frame their own constitution.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • Proposed Dominion status and a Constituent Assembly after World War II. • Right for any province to remain outside the Union (secession).
records.Constitutional Significance: First formal recognition by the British of India's right to frame its own constitution, though the proposal was rejected by major parties.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • Proposed a national interim executive council with equal representation for Congress and the Muslim League.
records.Constitutional Significance: Represented an attempt to resolve political deadlocks and move towards an interim Indian-led government.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • Recommended a scheme for a single Constituent Assembly (389 members) elected by provincial assemblies. • Proposed a three-tier structure (Union, Groups, and Provinces) and an interim government. • Rejected the demand for a separate Pakistan.
records.Constitutional Significance: Directly led to the formation of the Constituent Assembly that drafted the current Indian Constitution.

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Assembly of 299 members (after partition) convened.
• 6 Dec: Formal establishment; 9 Dec: First meeting (Sinha as temporary chair); 11 Dec: Election of Rajendra Prasad as permanent President.
records.Constitutional Significance: The formal body responsible for creating the sovereign legal framework of independent India.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • Defined the ideals of an Independent Sovereign Republic where power is derived from the people. • Promised social, economic, and political justice and equality.
records.Constitutional Significance: Provided the philosophical foundation for the Constitution and the legal framework for its Preamble.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • Proposed by the All India Women's Conference to ensure gender equality.
records.Constitutional Significance: Influenced the advocacy for social, economic, and political justice without reserved quotas for women in the draft Constitution.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • Proposed the partition of British India into two independent dominions: India and Pakistan. • Allowed princely states to choose which dominion to join.
records.Constitutional Significance: The definitive agreement that led to the legal partition and the immediate transfer of power.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • 29 August: Committee formed with Dr. Ambedkar as Chairman to prepare the final text of the Constitution.
records.Constitutional Significance: Tasked with synthesizing global principles and distinct Indian requirements into a final legal framework.

records.Main Provisions and Changes:
• Created two independent dominions: India and Pakistan, and abolished the office of Viceroy and Secretary of State.
• Abolished the British Crown’s sovereignty and suzerainty over princely states.
• Empowered Constituent Assemblies to act as fully sovereign bodies with both legislative and constituent functions.
records.Constitutional Significance: Formally ended British rule in India and transferred absolute sovereignty to the people through their Constituent Assemblies.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • 26 November: Final text adopted; immediate effect given to provisions on citizenship, elections, and provisional parliament.
records.Constitutional Significance: Marked the completion of the drafting process and formal acceptance of the supreme law of the land.

records.Main Provisions and Changes: • 26 January: The entire Constitution came into force, making India a Republic.
records.Constitutional Significance: Marked the final transition of India into a fully sovereign democratic republic.
